title: Competing anisotropies and phase transitions in the $q$-state clock model with a $p$-fold crystalline field
abstract: We study the two-dimensional $q$-state clock model in the presence of an additional $p$-fold symmetry-breaking crystalline field using Monte Carlo simulations. While the pure clock model exhibits Berezinskii--Kosterlitz--Thouless (BKT) transitions for sufficiently large $q$, the effect of competing discrete anisotropies on this topological phase remains nontrivial. We show that even weak crystalline fields qualitatively modify the phase diagram by suppressing the BKT phase and inducing transitions to states with true long-range order. The resulting behavior depends sensitively on the interplay between the intrinsic $\mathbb{Z}_q$ symmetry and the imposed $\mathbb{Z}_p$ anisotropy. In particular, in the six-state clock model for $p=2$ we observe qualitatively different scenarios depending on the sign of the field: a single transition for $h_2>0$ and a two-step ordering process for $h_2<0$ with an intermediate ordered phase. For $p=3$, the system exhibits a direct transition consistent with three-state Potts criticality. These results demonstrate that the phase structure cannot be inferred from symmetry considerations alone, but is governed by the competition between distinct locking mechanisms. Our findings provide a discrete counterpart to the multi-frequency sine-Gordon description of generalized $XY$ models and illustrate how additional anisotropies reshape topological phase transitions in two dimensions.
summary: This paper explores how adding a p-fold symmetry-breaking field to the 2D q-state clock model reshapes its phase diagram. It demonstrates that these fields can destroy the topological BKT phase and replace it with conventional long-range order, with the specific transition type depending on the competition between the intrinsic and imposed symmetries.
main_problem: Investigating how competing discrete anisotropies (intrinsic Zq symmetry vs. external Zp crystalline fields) affect the phase transitions and the stability of the BKT phase in the 2D q-state clock model.
main_result: Even weak crystalline fields suppress the BKT phase and induce transitions to long-range order, with the phase diagram being governed by the competition between locking mechanisms rather than symmetry alone. Specifically, for q=6, p=2, the sign of the field determines whether there is a single transition (h2 > 0) or a two-step ordering process (h2 < 0).
method: Monte Carlo simulations using the Metropolis algorithm combined with finite-size scaling (FSS) analysis to determine critical exponents and universality classes.
model_or_system: A two-dimensional q-state clock model on a square lattice with an additional p-fold crystalline field term, described by the Hamiltonian H = -J sum cos(theta_i - theta_j) - hp sum cos(p*theta_i).
key_observables: Specific heat, generalized magnetizations (m1, m3), magnetic susceptibilities, and logarithmic derivatives of magnetization moments.
important_parameters: Temperature (T/J), crystalline field strength (hp), symmetry indices (p, q), and lattice size (L).
main_assumptions: The study assumes the system is in the strong-anisotropy limit of generalized XY models and focuses on the interplay between discrete symmetries.
figures_summary: Figures show temperature dependence of specific heat, magnetization, and susceptibility for the isotropic model; phase diagrams and specific heat peaks for p=2 (showing single vs. double transitions); and FSS analysis of critical exponent ratios for p=3.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the model and the problem of competing anisotropies, details the Monte Carlo methodology, presents results for the p=2 case (distinguishing between positive and negative field strengths), analyzes the p=3 case (identifying Potts criticality), and concludes with a discussion on the instability of the BKT phase and the role of finite-size effects.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
